body_txt: Cinderella is perhaps one of the most famous fairy-tales there is, and it's a fairy-tale that has been around since 1st century Greece and the story of Rhodopis. Over the years, it has seen its fair share of versions and adaptations , one of the most popular being the one by Charles Perrault and later adapted by Disney in their version . As a result, it's a story that has proven so easy to tell and adapt that the basic outline has become something of a stock plot . Here are the main notes: Cinderella is a pretty girl with a Wicked Stepmother and two spoiled stepsisters. She's living a life in servitude to her stepfamily, who often neglect or mistreat her, often forcing her to sleep in the dirty fireplace covered in cinders (hence the character's Meaningful Name ). When opportunity arises to attend a royal ball, the stepfamily leaves Cindy at home, but she disguises herself (sometimes getting The Makeover ) and goes too. She meets and dances with the prince, but she's forced to flee before he can learn who she is. In her haste, she leaves something behind, traditionally one of her shoes . Because the prince has fallen in love with her, he takes this item and searches the kingdom for its owner . When at last he finds Cindy, he takes her away from her life of drudgery and marries her. Variations on the story exist: sometimes she gets to the ball through explicitly supernatural assistance, sometimes she gets there through friends or her own ingenuity, sometimes she already has the dress or dresses stashed away long before she needs them. There may be only one ball or there may be several ( three is common ). In some versions, it is the prince who gives her a token, and Cindy proves her identity by returning it. The story may be set in some medieval-ish time with a literal prince, or transposed to modern day with a male lead who is merely rich and 'princely'. And of course writers can give the supporting characters their own subplots. The recognizability of these stories means that the audience will still read it as 'Cinderella' even if a great deal of it has changed: as long as enough of the beats are kept , the rest of it can be entirely unique without confusing the reader. These stories actually seem to thrive on being re-cut and re-told in different styles; rather than continue to tell the same story, they stick to the basic idea while adding their own flavor. An example doesn't need to rehash the entire story in order to count. If Cinderella only has one stepsister and the story ends on a Be Yourself aesop, it's just as much a Cinderella story as the one where the stepfamily plot is skipped entirely in favor of a story about a girl going to a dance and running away, or the one where Cinderella never gets to go to the ball and the story ends in tragedy. And don't forget about all the parodies! With that said, these stories still need to be, well, stories, whether stand-alone or as part of a larger whole (often as part of a Fairy Tale Episode ). Minor references to Cinderella would go here instead. Also note that while this trope can allow for full adaptations, there still needs to be enough of a spin for it to be a distinct story; works that are just adaptations with nothing added or altered wouldn't count since at that point it's not a reference or template but a completely straight re-telling of the story, and thus not this trope. Instead that's a derivative work . If the character's name also follows the template, this may overlap with Ladyella . For another fairy-tale related plot trope, see Beanstalk Parody . Subtrope of Whole-Plot Reference .
